request.question: Sorry is a 1966 song that was written by Stevie Wright and another Australian musician who moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, but was born where?

title: Chris Cheney
George Young (rock musician)
Sorry (The Easybeats song)
Black Eyed Bruiser (album)
The Wrights (Australian band)
Wedding Ring (song)
She's So Fine
Evie (song)
Harry Bailey
Hard Road (Stevie Wright album)
sentences: Christopher John Cheney (born 2 January 1975) is an Australian rock musician, record producer and studio owner.
He is the founding mainstay guitarist, songwriter and lead vocalist of the psychobilly band, The Living End, which was formed in 1994 with school mate Scott Owen.

Cheney wrote the group's top 20 hits on the ARIA Singles Chart: "Second Solution" / "Prisoner of Society" (1997), "All Torn Down" (1999), "Pictures in the Mirror" (2000), "Roll On" (2001), "One Said to the Other" (2003), "What's on Your Radio" (2005), "Wake Up" (2006) and "White Noise" (2008).
In 2004 Cheney joined the super group The Wrights which put out a cover version of Stevie Wright's epic 11-minute track, "Evie" as a single.
At the APRA Awards of 2009 Cheney won 'Song of the Year' for writing The Living End's track, "White Noise".
In 2005 he married Emma, the couple have two daughters and are co-owners of a recording facility, Red Door Studios.
In 2011 the Cheney family relocated to Los Angeles.
George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.
Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.

He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).
Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.
"Sorry" is a 1966 song and single by Australian rock group The Easybeats, which was written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright.
It peaked at #1 on the Australian Go-Set's National Top 40 in mid November 1966.
It remained at #1 on the Australian Charts for 2 weeks in November 1966.
Black Eyed Bruiser is the second studio album from Australian singer Stevie Wright.
The album was not as commercially successful as its predecessor "Hard Road" and would be the Wright's final album released with production team Vanda and Young and record label Albert Productions.
The Wrights were a one-off Australian rock music "supergroup".

They consisted of Nic Cester (of Jet), Bernard Fanning (of Powderfinger), Phil Jamieson (of Grinspoon), Kram (of Spiderbait), Chris Cheney (of The Living End), Davey Lane (of You Am I and The Pictures) and Pat Bourke (of Dallas Crane).
They are named after Australian music legend and former Easybeats frontman Stevie Wright, the original performer of the song-trilogy "Evie" which was the group's feature song.
"Wedding Ring" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.
It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached #6 on the Australian charts.
"She's So Fine" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.
It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached number three in the Australian charts.
"Evie" is an Australian rock song released as a single in 1974 by Australian singer and former frontman of The Easybeats, Stevie Wright.
It was written by Harry Vanda and George Young.
It has been suggested that it is the first 11-minute song to chart at #1 anywhere in the world.
Harry Richard Bailey (29 October 1922, Picton, New South Wales – 8 September 1985, Mount White, New South Wales) was an Australian psychiatrist and hospital administrator.

He bore the primary responsibility for treatment of mental patients and drug dependent clients via deep sleep therapy and other method at a mental hospital in the Sydney North Shore suburb of Pennant Hills; he was its principal.
The treatment has been linked with the deaths of a total of 85 patients, including 19 who committed suicide.
Bailey committed suicide while he was investigated.

One of Bailey's clients was Australian rock and pop singer and songwriter Stevie Wright, who was being treated for methadone addiction and later committed suicide, a report that was later part of a 2013 documentary on "Australian Story".
Hard Road is the debut solo album from Australian singer Stevie Wright.
The album's first single "Evie (Part 1)" was hugely successful and the title track was later covered on Rod Stewart's 1974 album "Smiler".
The album itself reach #2 on the Australian albums charts in 1974 was the 16th highest selling album in Australia that year.
The compact disc is currently out-of-print and has become quite rare.
A digital edition was available on iTunes as of June, 2014.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must be followed exactly):
1) Output format: return a single string in the `summary` field. That string must be a compact, structured summary containing these three labeled sections in this exact order and spelling:  
   First-hop evidence:  
   Bridge needed:  
   Retrieval direction:  
Each section should be short (each 1–3 short clauses or sentences) and the whole summary should be concise.

2) First-hop evidence: extract and report the concrete fact(s) present in the provided `passages` that are relevant to the question. Name the passage title(s) you used and give a very short quoted phrase or paraphrase (<=15 words) showing the supporting fact. Preserve the concise factual style (e.g., "Alan Ruck — born July 1, 1956" or "Passage: 'Ferris Bueller' cast lists Alan Ruck"). Do not invent facts.

3) Bridge needed: explicitly state the single overlap, entity, or missing fact that the next retrieval hop must verify to answer the question (for example: the person to check for cast membership; the named tournament page to fetch; the album title whose release year is needed). If the passages already name a candidate title or person, you may cite that candidate here as the bridge to be verified, but do not treat or phrase it as the final answer.

4) Retrieval direction: give ordered, concrete next retrieval actions (1., 2., ...) or one-line query templates the next agent should use to verify the bridge and obtain the missing fact. Examples: fetch the page titled exactly "<Tournament Title>" and inspect its 'Venues'/'Schedule' sections; retrieve the film/TV cast list for "<Work>"; search "<Person name>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org. Keep steps explicit and in order. Do not ask for external sources beyond retrieving the named pages or running the stated queries.

5) Prohibitions and constraints: do NOT produce a final answer to the question or assert that the bridge overlap is confirmed. Do NOT invent or infer missing facts. Avoid long verbatim excerpts from sources (keep quotes <=15 words). If a passage supports a short candidate label (a single title or name), do not let that stand alone as the summary — still include the three labeled sections and a retrieval plan. Keep language neutral and factual. 

6) When to include additional guidance (examples of minimal behaviors to follow):
   - If a passage names a tournament/work that appears to be the bridge, put that exact title in 'Bridge needed:' and instruct fetching that page and which sections (Venues/Matches/Schedule/Cast) to check. 
   - If the passages identify a person (e.g., an assassin or credited actor) as the bridge, name that person in 'Bridge needed:' and provide a short retrieval query pattern (e.g., '"<Name>" birthplace site:wikipedia.org' or 'fetch "<Name>" biography').
   - If a passage lists some but not all of a question's film credits for an actor, state which film titles from the question are confirmed in 'First-hop evidence:' and which are unverified in 'Bridge needed:', then give queries to fetch the missing film/actor pages.

7) Preserve concise, passage-grounded evidence extraction and always make the next-hop actionable. The `summary` must not change the input/output field names.

Return only the `summary` string following the rules above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the field `query`.

Requirements for the `query` output:
- Return exactly one compact retrieval query string as the value of `query`. Do not output anything else (no explanations, no answers, no extra fields).
- Keep the query short and focused (one concise line). Avoid numbered lists, imperative prefixes (e.g., "Fetch:"), multi-step plans, extraction checklists, or long procedural text.
- Never emit multiple separate queries or parallel site: filters in place of a single focused query. Do not include downstream instructions to the retriever.

How to construct the single compact query (choose the rule that best fits the provided inputs):
1) Title-first verification (when summary_1 names a specific page title):
   - Begin the query with the page title tokens taken from summary_1, then append a very short natural-language verification phrase asking for the specific fact to confirm. Keep the whole line compact. Example shape: <Page Title> what is this commonly called in the United Kingdom
2) Film + actor / birth-date check (when summary_1 or question indicates a candidate actor from a film):
   - Put the film title and the single candidate actor name from summary_1 together, then append a short confirmatory request: confirm they starred in the film and give birth date. Example shape: <Film Title> <Actor Name> confirm starred in the film and birth date
3) Person occupations / credits (when summary_1 requests occupations or filmography for a named person):
   - Make a concise keyword-style query combining the person name, occupation/filmography keywords and any specific titles from summary_1. Example shape: <Person Name> filmography occupations "Title A" "Title B"
4) Wikipedia-focused exceptions (only if summary_1 or the question explicitly indicates the need for Wikipedia pages, e.g., contains 'HTO', 'WW', or explicitly asks for Wikipedia biography/infobox evidence):
   - Include site:wikipedia.org in the single compact query and add tight terms such as creator OR designer OR biography OR "release date" as needed. Still keep one compact query line.

General constraints (always apply):
- Prefer using tokens that already appear in summary_1 as anchors (page titles, film titles, person names). Do not invent new entity names.
- Do not include the final answer, candidate answers, or verbatim page content in the query.
- Avoid broad or noisy terms, multi-line/site-split queries, and retrieval operator clutter. Keep wording minimal and unambiguous so a retriever can fetch the canonical page(s).
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Keep the native I/O exactly: return only the single `summary` string field. Work only from facts present in `question`, `context`, and `passages`. Do not add information that is not present in those inputs.

General integration rule (default): If the `context` asserts a factual claim and one or more `passages` corroborate that claim, the `summary` must (a) restate the claim succinctly, (b) name at least one supporting passage by its title, and (c) include at most one very short confirming fragment or brief quoted clause taken directly from that passage (no long verbatim spans). Keep wording concise (usually 1–3 short sentences) and preserve factual tokens exactly as they appear in the inputs when reporting names, numbers, or titles.

Additional conditional rules (apply only when their triggers are met):
- Bridge/second-hop corroboration + retention: When the `context` asserts a first-hop claim that should be verified by a second-hop passage (for example: the context claims an actor appeared in Film A and Film B and one of the `passages` explicitly corroborates presence in Film B), produce an integrated-evidence summary of 1–3 short sentences that (1) briefly restates the first-hop claim from `context`, (2) explicitly names the corroborating second-hop passage title (either inline or in a simple parenthetical containing only the title) and may include at most one very short confirming fragment from that passage, and (3) append the short retention sentence: "Retain these facts for the final-answer agent." Do not invent facts; remain concise.

- Role-confirmation (game/subject passage + biographical passage present): When the question asks which person performed a role (e.g., "which managing editor ... calling the action") and the inputs include (A) a game/subject passage listing commentators/participants and (B) a biographical passage giving a candidate's occupation/role, override the terse entity-label rule and produce a role-confirmation style `summary` of 1–3 short sentences: (1) one short clause restating the claim to confirm; (2) two micro-evidence clauses, each a short paraphrase that names the supporting passage title in parentheses (one for the game/subject passage, one for the biographical passage); (3) one integrated sentence linking the person's full name and the occupation/role from the passages to the asserted role (that they called the action). If the pipeline needs the fact preserved for later, append: "Retain this fact for final-answer agent." Use at most one very short quoted fragment if helpful; do not add information not present in the inputs.

- Two-candidate biographical integration: When the question names two candidate people and `passages` include explicit supporting biographical passages for both candidates (both passage titles for the two names appear in `passages`), override the terse-label output and produce 1–2 short sentences that integrate both hops: give one brief clause/sentence paraphrasing each person with a parenthetical passage title citation, optionally include at most one very short quoted fragment (≤ ~6 words) to confirm a notable item (e.g., a film title), and finish with a concise link identifying which candidate matches the phrasing of the question. If only one candidate is supported, fall back to the canonical-name label rule.

- Temporal "When did" + genre-change diagnostics: If the question begins with a temporal ask (e.g., "When did...") and the `passages` include an explicit genre label token (e.g., "rap rock") plus multiple candidate dates or passages describing a change in sound, produce a compact diagnostic `summary` (≈1–3 short sentences) that (a) restates the genre claim and cites at least one passage title, (b) reports formation year(s) with their sourcing and briefly note any discrepancy, (c) reports the debut-album title and release date with its passage title, and (d) mentions any later passage documenting a shift/expansion in sound with its title. End with one short sentence naming the remaining evidentiary gap needed to answer the question precisely (for example: "The earliest explicit attribution of 'rap rock' is not given here; check formation year(s), the debut release date, and contemporary press circa 1997–1999."). Do not resolve the gap or invent facts.

- U.S. states entity-label exception: If the question asks which U.S. state(s) (phrases like "which US states", "which U.S. states", or "which states") and the provided `passages` explicitly list state names, output only the canonical state name(s) exactly as they appear in the sources, comma-separated with no surrounding sentence framing or trailing punctuation. Example: "Virginia, West Virginia". Do not include passage titles, explanatory clauses, or retention notes in this case.

- Prefer explicit-support single-source attribution: When `context` asserts a claim and at least one `passage` explicitly states that same claim (i.e., there is an explicit-support passage alongside other metadata-only passages), prefer a single concise sentence that attributes the claim to that explicit-support passage. Name the explicit-support passage title (either inline or in a simple parenthetical containing only the title) and avoid appending metadata-only passages unless necessary to resolve ambiguity required downstream.

Grounding, quoting, and conservatism: Always cite supporting passage titles when they corroborate a `context` claim. Include at most one very short quoted fragment or brief confirming clause from a cited passage; do not include long verbatim quotations. Preserve exact tokens from the inputs when reporting names, numbers, titles, or aliases. If the passages resolve identity ambiguity, present the resolved identification directly; if identity is unresolved, explicitly note the ambiguity and what evidence is missing.

Formatting and hard prohibitions:
- Do not invent facts or expand beyond the provided inputs.
- Do not output fields other than `summary`.
- Do not begin the `summary` with a single-word answer token such as 'Yes.' or 'No.'.
- Do not issue second-person commands, retrieval/fetch/verify instructions, or direct the pipeline/user to consult external pages. If the text you would generate would otherwise include such an instruction, instead state declaratively that the supplied `passages` do not corroborate the claim (e.g., "The supplied passages do not corroborate the founding year.").
- Avoid parenthetical evidence blocks that combine a passage title and a quoted fragment like '(PassageTitle — "...")'; instead name the passage title inline or use a simple parenthetical containing only the title, and place any very short quoted fragment as a brief confirming clause outside such compound parentheses.

Keep summaries concise (usually 1–3 short sentences) and return only the single `summary` string field.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

Maintain all base behaviors and the numbered rules 1–7 in the original specification (general minimal-answer rule; agreement extraction; patterned question triggers; nationality questions; two-performer composition rule; narrow assumed referent; formatting prohibitions; and the additional A–G adjustments). Apply the following small, ordered set of additional, narrowly scoped behaviors (these are supplemental to and constrained by the base rules; apply them only when their listed triggers match):

Order-of-checks and special-case overrides (apply in this order):
- Step 0 (explicit retrieval/bridge signal): If either visible summary contains an explicit retrieval/bridge instruction or request (signals such as the phrases 'Bridge needed', 'Fetch', 'Fetch the page', 'retrieval', an enumerated retrieval plan, or similarly explicit language asking for further page fetches or bridging), do not emit a definitive factual minimal answer even if the summaries otherwise appear to agree. Instead emit a concise permission/clarifying yes/no request (one short question sentence asking to perform the requested retrieval, e.g., 'Shall I fetch the requested pages to confirm?') as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not include any explanatory text or additional tokens.

- Step 1 (exact agreement on a short named-entity): If summary_1 and summary_2 present the same short named-entity string (case-insensitive match, 1–5 words) that unambiguously answers the question per the base rules, do not produce a clarification or a lookup question. Emit that agreed minimal token/phrase directly (trim surrounding punctuation/whitespace and preserve internal capitalization) as the only content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. This enforces the base rule that agreement yields a direct minimal answer.

- Step 2 (narrow internal-knowledge allowances): In these strictly constrained, high-confidence circumstances you may consult internal background knowledge only as specified below; otherwise continue to rely solely on the visible summaries and fall back to clarification when uncertain.
  - Nationality/demonym override: Trigger: the question explicitly asks for a nationality/demonym and the visible summaries unambiguously identify a single named person as the referent (a single named person appears in the summaries and there is no ambiguity about who is being asked about). Behavior: if you have high internal confidence in that person's widely known nationality/demonym, emit the single demonym token/short phrase as the minimal answer inside [[ ## answer ## ]]. If you do NOT have high internal confidence, emit a concise clarification question (as the minimal answer) instead. Never append qualifiers, parentheticals, or sources.
  - Shared-illustrator override: Trigger: summary_1 lists one or more specific book titles (multiple candidate titles) and summary_2 indicates no illustrator credits in the supplied passages. Behavior: if the listed candidate books are canonical entries from the same series and you have high internal confidence that a single credited illustrator is consistently credited for all those specific titles, emit that illustrator's name as the sole minimal answer inside [[ ## answer ## ]]. If you lack high confidence or the titles have differing credited illustrators, do not apply this override and instead emit a brief clarification question.

- Step 3 (demonyms comparison for 'Are X and Y from the same country?'): Trigger: the question matches the pattern 'Are X and Y from the same country?' and both summaries contain one demonym token for each named person. Behavior: if both demonym tokens are present and they differ under case-insensitive comparison, emit a single-word negation token (e.g., No) as the entire content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. If they are identical (case-insensitive), emit that common demonym verbatim per the nationality rule. If either demonym is missing, follow the base prompt behavior (ask a concise clarification or apply other base rules).

- Step 4 (strip leading causal 'for '): Trigger: if the minimal answer you would emit begins with the leading causal preposition 'for ' followed by a short phrase that appears verbatim in at least one visible summary, remove the leading 'for ' and emit only the remainder as the minimal answer inside [[ ## answer ## ]]. Apply this rewrite only at final formatting.

Additional constraints and safeguards (always apply):
- Preserve the base prompt's strict output contract: the [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only the minimal answer string (or a brief clarification question when required), with no added punctuation, explanatory text, provenance, or surrounding characters.
- Do not invent facts beyond the narrowly allowed internal-knowledge overrides; when in doubt about high internal confidence for an allowed override, emit a concise clarification question instead of guessing.
- Do not append qualifiers, parentheticals, nationality tags, or type labels to an extracted minimal substring; emit exactly the substring normalized only by trimming surrounding punctuation/whitespace (subject to the parenthetical handling rules A–C from the base prompt).
- When a visible summary explicitly contains a short creator claim (phrases like 'created by' or 'series creator' immediately followed by a short name) and there is no contradictory creator in the other summary, treat that claim as sufficient to extract and emit the short name as the minimal answer (per the base prompt's explicit-creator delta).
- The explicit-retrieval/bridge check (Step 0) takes precedence over the 'emit-agreement' rule: if a summary requests retrieval/bridging, ask permission to fetch rather than returning a factual minimal answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by these rules (or a short clarification/permission question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

sentences: Christopher John Cheney (born 2 January 1975) is an Australian rock musician, record producer and studio owner.
He is the founding mainstay guitarist, songwriter and lead vocalist of the psychobilly band, The Living End, which was formed in 1994 with school mate Scott Owen.

Cheney wrote the group's top 20 hits on the ARIA Singles Chart: "Second Solution" / "Prisoner of Society" (1997), "All Torn Down" (1999), "Pictures in the Mirror" (2000), "Roll On" (2001), "One Said to the Other" (2003), "What's on Your Radio" (2005), "Wake Up" (2006) and "White Noise" (2008).
In 2004 Cheney joined the super group The Wrights which put out a cover version of Stevie Wright's epic 11-minute track, "Evie" as a single.
At the APRA Awards of 2009 Cheney won 'Song of the Year' for writing The Living End's track, "White Noise".
In 2005 he married Emma, the couple have two daughters and are co-owners of a recording facility, Red Door Studios.
In 2011 the Cheney family relocated to Los Angeles.
George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.
Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.

He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).
Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.
"Sorry" is a 1966 song and single by Australian rock group The Easybeats, which was written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright.
It peaked at #1 on the Australian Go-Set's National Top 40 in mid November 1966.
It remained at #1 on the Australian Charts for 2 weeks in November 1966.
Black Eyed Bruiser is the second studio album from Australian singer Stevie Wright.
The album was not as commercially successful as its predecessor "Hard Road" and would be the Wright's final album released with production team Vanda and Young and record label Albert Productions.
The Wrights were a one-off Australian rock music "supergroup".

They consisted of Nic Cester (of Jet), Bernard Fanning (of Powderfinger), Phil Jamieson (of Grinspoon), Kram (of Spiderbait), Chris Cheney (of The Living End), Davey Lane (of You Am I and The Pictures) and Pat Bourke (of Dallas Crane).
They are named after Australian music legend and former Easybeats frontman Stevie Wright, the original performer of the song-trilogy "Evie" which was the group's feature song.
"Wedding Ring" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.
It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached #6 on the Australian charts.
"She's So Fine" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.
It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached number three in the Australian charts.
"Evie" is an Australian rock song released as a single in 1974 by Australian singer and former frontman of The Easybeats, Stevie Wright.
It was written by Harry Vanda and George Young.
It has been suggested that it is the first 11-minute song to chart at #1 anywhere in the world.
Harry Richard Bailey (29 October 1922, Picton, New South Wales – 8 September 1985, Mount White, New South Wales) was an Australian psychiatrist and hospital administrator.

He bore the primary responsibility for treatment of mental patients and drug dependent clients via deep sleep therapy and other method at a mental hospital in the Sydney North Shore suburb of Pennant Hills; he was its principal.
The treatment has been linked with the deaths of a total of 85 patients, including 19 who committed suicide.
Bailey committed suicide while he was investigated.

One of Bailey's clients was Australian rock and pop singer and songwriter Stevie Wright, who was being treated for methadone addiction and later committed suicide, a report that was later part of a 2013 documentary on "Australian Story".
Hard Road is the debut solo album from Australian singer Stevie Wright.
The album's first single "Evie (Part 1)" was hugely successful and the title track was later covered on Rod Stewart's 1974 album "Smiler".
The album itself reach #2 on the Australian albums charts in 1974 was the 16th highest selling album in Australia that year.
The compact disc is currently out-of-print and has become quite rare.
A digital edition was available on iTunes as of June, 2014.

response.hop1_query: Sorry is a 1966 song that was written by Stevie Wright and another Australian musician who moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, but was born where?
hop1_docs: Sorry (The Easybeats song) | "Sorry" is a 1966 song and single by Australian rock group The Easybeats, which was written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright.  It peaked at #1 on the Australian Go-Set's National Top 40 in mid November 1966.  It remained at #1 on the Australian Charts for 2 weeks in November 1966.

Stevie Wright | Stephen Carlton "Stevie" Wright (20 December 1947 – 27 December 2015), formerly billed as Little Stevie, was an English-born musician and songwriter who has been called Australia's first international pop star.  During 1964–69 he was lead singer of Sydney-based rock and roll band the Easybeats, widely regarded as the greatest Australian pop band of the 1960s.

Kram (musician) | Kram (born Mark Anthony Maher in Finley, New South Wales) is the drummer/singer of Australian band Spiderbait.  His stage name is an anagram of his first name; "Kram" is simply "Mark" spelled backwards.  Kram has appeared at the 2004 benefit concert for the South-East Asian tsunami, WaveAid, as a member of supergroup The Wrights who performed Stevie Wright's 3 part classic, "Evie".

A Place in the Sun (Stevie Wonder song) | "A Place in the Sun" is a 1966 soul single by American and Motown musician Stevie Wonder.  Written by Ronald Miller and Bryan Wells, it was one of Wonder's first songs to contain social commentary.  "A Place in the Sun" was his third Top Ten hit since 1963 and hit #9 on the "Billboard" pop singles chart and # 3 on the R&B charts.  Stevie Wonder also recorded a version of the song in Italian titled "Il Sole è di Tutti" ("The Sun is for Everyone").

George Young (rock musician) | George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.  Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.  He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).  Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.

Wedding Ring (song) | "Wedding Ring" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.  It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached #6 on the Australian charts.

Evie (song) | "Evie" is an Australian rock song released as a single in 1974 by Australian singer and former frontman of The Easybeats, Stevie Wright.  It was written by Harry Vanda and George Young.  It has been suggested that it is the first 11-minute song to chart at #1 anywhere in the world.
hop1_titles: A Place in the Sun (Stevie Wonder song)
response.summary_1:
First-hop evidence:
- "Sorry (The Easybeats song)" — "written by George Young and Stevie Wright"
- "George Young (rock musician)" — "born in Scotland; moved to Australia as a teenager"

Bridge needed:
- George Young — verify his stated birthplace (confirm "born in Scotland" or find the specific town)

Retrieval direction:
1. Fetch the page titled "George Young (rock musician)" and inspect the lead/infobox 'Born' and 'Early life' sections for birthplace.
2. If the page is ambiguous, run query: "George Redburn Young birthplace site:wikipedia.org" and check cited reliable biography or reference on that page.
hop2_docs: George Young (rock musician) | George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.  Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.  He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).  Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.

Alexander Young (musician) | Alexander Young (28 December 1938 – 4 August 1997), also known as George Alexander, was a Scottish singer, songwriter, saxophonist, bassist, guitarist and session musician.  He is an elder brother of George Young, the rhythm guitarist and founding member of the Easybeats, as well as Malcolm and Angus Young, founding members of the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, and the younger brother of Stephen Young, the father of Stevie Young, who was also a member of AC/DC.
Eurovision Young Musicians 2018 | The Eurovision Young Musicians 2018 will be the nighteenth edition of the Eurovision Young Musicians contest.  So far 3 countries have confirmed their participation.

Whitney Young Birthplace and Museum | The Whitney Young Birthplace and Museum was the birthplace and childhood home of Whitney M. Young, Jr., an American civil rights leader.  The simple wooden house in Shelby County, Kentucky, near Louisville, is on the campus of the former Lincoln Institute, an all-black high school that Young attended and Young's father led.
John Young (British musician) | John Young (born 31 May 1956) is a British rock musician from Liverpool.  He is currently the keyboardist and singer for the progressive rock band Lifesigns.
Robert Young (musician) | Robert "Throb" Young (1964 – 9 September 2014) was a Scottish musician.  He was a member of the alternative rock band Primal Scream from 1984 to 2006.

Music of Texas | The U.S. state of Texas has long been a center for musical innovation and is the birthplace of many notable musicians.  Texans have pioneered developments in Tejano and Conjunto music, Rock 'n Roll, Western swing, jazz, punk rock, country, hip-hop, electronic music, gothic industrial music, religious music, mariachi, psychedelic rock, zydeco and the blues.
